title: Iran Didn’t Win the War
gist: Jeffrey argues that despite Washington's scorn for the imperfect Iran cease-fire, the cumulative three-year regional campaign culminating in Operation Epic Fury left Iran far weaker, its proxy network in ruins and nuclear and conventional base decimated; Iran's lone win, closing the Strait of Hormuz, is a wasting asset, and the US holds leverage to lock in containment.

insights.claim: The February 28 Iran campaign should be read not in isolation but as the latest round of a continuous three-year regional war begun by Hamas's October 2023 attack, spanning Gaza, Lebanon, the toppling of Assad, and Red Sea battles with the Houthis.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: Judging the war by its narrow stated goals (regime change, ending the nuclear threat) understates a cumulative strategic shift against Iran.
insights.quote: Epic Fury is only the latest round of an ongoing international armed conflict with Iran.
insights.locator: THE BIG PICTURE / 'The military campaign that the United States and Israel launched'

insights.claim: The cumulative campaign has largely reversed Iran's regional successes of the prior 20 years, leaving the US and partners in a far stronger Middle East position than before October 2023.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: The strategic balance in the Middle East has shifted durably toward the US bloc, a backdrop for regional risk pricing.
insights.quote: the United States has accumulated a series of gains that have largely reversed Iran's regional successes over the prior 20 years
insights.locator: Closing / 'But over the past three years'

insights.claim: Iran's proxy network has totally collapsed: Hamas held a Gaza cease-fire, Iraqi militias and the Houthis largely stayed on the sidelines, Iraq rejected pro-Iranian PM candidates, and Israel decisively defeated Hezbollah and now holds Lebanese territory to the Litani River.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: The 'Axis of Resistance' as a coordinated deterrent is broken, reducing Iran's ability to threaten regional energy and shipping via proxies.
insights.quote: Tehran's proxy network, weakened in the past three years, has now totally collapsed.
insights.locator: FORWARD PROGRESS / 'But even with legitimate qualms'

insights.claim: Since February 28 the US struck more than 1,500 Iranian air-defense targets and 1,250 drone and ballistic-missile storage facilities, and Iran estimates $270 billion in war damage, decimating its conventional and air-defense capabilities.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: Iran's degraded air defenses leave it exposed to renewed strikes, a credible coercive threat underpinning US negotiating leverage.
insights.quote: the United States has hit more than 1,500 Iranian air defense targets and 1,250 drone and ballistic missile storage facilities
insights.locator: FORWARD PROGRESS / 'The war also destroyed much of Iran's remaining military capabilities'

insights.claim: Iran was mostly ignored by its supposed allies Beijing and Moscow during the conflict, exposing the limits of those partnerships when Tehran was under direct attack.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: The China-Russia-Iran axis offers Tehran little hard backing in a crisis, relevant to assessing any future escalation.
insights.quote: Tehran has been mostly ignored by its supposed allies in Beijing and Moscow
insights.locator: Opening / 'Iran's proxy network of militant groups is largely in ruins'

insights.claim: Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz raised oil prices only ~50 percent, far less than the 1973-74 embargo's 300%+ spike, because states quickly found workarounds.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: The oil-shock channel is more muted than feared; supply elasticity has risen, capping the energy-price tail risk from Gulf conflict.
insights.quote: oil prices rose only about 50 percent since the war started
insights.locator: FORWARD PROGRESS / 'Iran's successful closure of the Strait of Hormuz'

insights.claim: Workarounds blunted the Hormuz closure: US crude exports hit a record 5.6 million bpd in May, Saudi Arabia moved up to 7 million bpd (a third of Gulf exports) via bypass pipeline, and the UAE is near completion of a pipeline doubling its overland capacity past 3 million bpd.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: Bypass infrastructure and US shale structurally reduce Hormuz's chokepoint leverage; a durable de-rating of Gulf-disruption risk.
insights.quote: reaching a record high level of crude oil exports of 5.6 million barrels per day in May
insights.locator: FORWARD PROGRESS / 'When Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz this year'

insights.claim: The Hormuz blockade is a wasting asset because it harms Iran itself, accelerates a global shift away from Gulf hydrocarbons, and Iran's limited export routes leave it more vulnerable to future blockades than its adversaries.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: Iran's one coercive lever depreciates over time and may backfire by speeding diversification away from its own oil.
insights.quote: which makes the blockade a wasting asset
insights.locator: FORWARD PROGRESS / 'Iran's oil embargo is also promoting'

themes.theme: Nuclear leverage and the JCPOA debate

insights.claim: The US is in a better bargaining position than under the 2015 JCPOA, because biting post-2018 sanctions plus the destruction of Iran's nuclear infrastructure let Washington trade cease-fire and sanctions relief for enrichment limits, whereas the JCPOA's restrictions were set to expire starting this year.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: Counters the 'we're no better off than 2015' critique; leverage from sanctions plus credible strike threat is the actionable containment tool.
insights.quote: The United States now finds itself in a better bargaining position than it would have been in if it had stayed in the JCPOA.
insights.locator: THE REAL DEAL / 'The United States now finds itself in a better bargaining position'

insights.claim: The memorandum of understanding only commits Iran to discuss its nuclear program and dilute its 60%-enriched stockpiles (close to the 90% weapons threshold and largely buried underground), with enrichment limits informally linked to sanctions relief.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: The nuclear outcome remains unresolved; the real test of the campaign is whether stockpiles are eliminated and future enrichment blocked.
insights.quote: Iran committed only to discussing its nuclear program, not to taking specific proactive steps other than diluting its stockpiles of 60 percent enriched uranium
insights.locator: THE REAL DEAL / 'Under the memorandum of understanding'

insights.claim: The decision to attack on February 28 failed as a 'total victory' because the administration overreached with regime-change-style ambitions, ignored decades of Hormuz-closure planning, and underestimated the difficulty of overthrowing ideological foes.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: Overambitious, underresourced war aims produced avoidable costs; the realistic goal was containment, not regime overthrow.
insights.quote: The problem was with the overly ambitious belief that the administration could achieve a total victory
insights.locator: THE BIG PICTURE / 'The only question for Washington'

insights.claim: US allies clashed with Washington (Gulf states blocked some air operations, Europe protested the lack of consultation) but are likely to reconcile rather than seek new security arrangements because they have few alternatives to the US against the residual Iranian threat.
insights.speaker: James F. Jeffrey
insights.so_what: US alliances in the Middle East are sticky despite friction, a contrast to the European drift toward autonomy described elsewhere.
insights.locator: THE REAL DEAL / 'But U.S. allies and partners are likely to work through'
